coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive detection of tetracycline in animal-origin foods using self-nitrogen doped carbon dots as fluorescent probes
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Mingying
Wu, Danni
Deng, Xiaoye
Ma, Junjie
Fan, Hui
Huang, Xiangru
Wang, Peiwen
Li, Yingjie
Liao, Xiufen
Si, Lulu
Chen, Congjin
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we successfully synthesized self-nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) from shrimp shells (waste biomass) via a hydrothermal process for tetracycline (TC) residue analysis in food. They exhibited excellent photostability, low toxicity, and a high fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of 9.72 %. Owing to the internal filtration effect (IFE) and static quenching effect (SQE), the fluorescence emission intensity of the N-CDs-based probe exhibited a marked decline concomitant with the augmentation of TC concentration. Under optimal circumstances, there was a robust linear relationship between the TC concentration (0–60 μM) and the system's degree of fluorescence quenching ((F0-F)/F0), with a low detection limit of 96 nM. The developed fluorescent probe was effectively applied to detect TC in milk and egg samples, achieving a relative standard deviation from 1.4 % to 3.2 % and recoveries from 98.2 % to 109.7 %, comparable to the standard high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) method. In conclusion, shrimp shell-derived N-CDs hold great potential as fluorescent probes for highly sensitive, selective, accurate and real-time TC detection, which is important in ensuring food safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Self-nitrogen doped carbon dots
Tetracycline
Shrimp shells
Fluorescence quenching
Food safety

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31660183), the Guangxi Natural Science Foundation (No. 2025GXNSFAA139023), the Dean/Opening Project of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Petrochemical Resource Processing and Process Intensification Technology (2024K007). The authors would like to thank Jianliang Liao from Shiyania Lab ( www.shiyanjia.com ) for the XPS measurement.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31660183), the Guangxi Natural Science Foundation (2025GXNSFAA069247), the Dean/Opening Project of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Petrochemical Resource Processing and Process Intensification Technology (2024K002). The authors would like to thank Jianliang Liao from Shiyania Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS measurement.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram of the preparation process of N-CDs.
The as-synthesized N-CDs' (A) TEM image (inset: lattice spacing of 0.212 nm under HRTEM), (B) particle size distribution, (C) XRD image, and (D) FT-IR spectra.
(A) XPS full-survey spectra of N-CDs and high-resolution XPS spectra of C1S (B), N1S (C), and O1S (D) for the N-CDs.

(A) UV-Vis absorption, emission, and excitation spectra of N-CDs (inset: pictures of N-CDs in daylight and under 365 nm UV light). (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs excited between 300 and 450 nm (inset: normalized fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of N- CDs in the presence of different concentrations of TC (insets: images of N-CDs and N-CDs/TC mixtures under 365 nm UV light, respectively). (B) The (F0-F)/ F0 values versus TC concentration (inset: the linear relationship between (F0-F)/ F0 values and TC concentration in the range of 0–60 μM). (C) Selectivity and (D) anti-interference testing.

(A) The excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs and UV-Vis adsorption spectrum of TC. (B) UV-Vis absorption spectra of N-CDs, TC, and TC/N-CDs solutions. Fluorescence lifetime spectra of (C) N-CDs and (D) N-CDs/TC solutions. (E) Mechanistic diagram of the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs by TC.
Results of the TC spiked assay in milk & eggs (n = 6).

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive detection of tetracycline in animal-origin foods using self-nitrogen doped carbon dots as fluorescent probes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Technical Center of Nanning Customs District, Nanning 530021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Technical Center of Nanning Customs District

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Technical Center of Nanning Customs District, Nanning 530021, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we successfully synthesized self-nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) from shrimp shells (waste biomass) via a hydrothermal process for tetracycline (TC) residue analysis in food. They exhibited excellent photostability, low toxicity, and a high fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of 9.72 %. Owing to the internal filtration effect (IFE) and static quenching effect (SQE), the fluorescence emission intensity of the N-CDs-based probe exhibited a marked decline concomitant with the augmentation of TC concentration. Under optimal circumstances, there was a robust linear relationship between the TC concentration (0–60 μM) and the system's degree of fluorescence quenching ((F0-F)/F0), with a low detection limit of 96 nM. The developed fluorescent probe was effectively applied to detect TC in milk and egg samples, achieving a relative standard deviation from 1.4 % to 3.2 % and recoveries from 98.2 % to 109.7 %, comparable to the standard high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) method. In conclusion, shrimp shell-derived N-CDs hold great potential as fluorescent probes for highly sensitive, selective, accurate and real-time TC detection, which is important in ensuring food safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline (TC) has been widely utilized as a main antibiotic for the prevention and treatment of microbiological diseases in animals due to its high antibacterial activity and low cost (Du et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2022). However, overuse of TC in animal husbandry leads to its accumulation in animals and residues in animal-derived foods like dairy products and eggs (Kaur et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2023). Consumption of such food can harm bone and tooth formation, damage internal organs, and cause the emergence of superbugs—drug-resistant bacteria that destroy the body's natural flora and reduce immunity (Chen et al., 2019). Thus, TC in food is a major concern. There are many methods for TC determination, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Feng et al., 2016), capillary electrophoresis (Prado et al., 2021), enzyme-linked immunoassay (Wang et al., 2017), colorimetric (Tang et al., 2022b), liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) (Moreno-González and García-Campaña, 2017) and fluorescence methods (Zhang et al., 2022). Despite their high accuracy and low limit of detection (LOD), these methods are not appropriate for fast and real-time tests because of their complicated sample pre-processing, time-consuming and laborious processes, costly equipment, and propensity for false positives. Consequently, creating a practical, affordable, and sensitive method for identifying TC residues in animal-derived food has become an urgent problem. The fluorescence detection methodology has burgeoned into a prevalently embraced technique for TC sensing, attributable to its virtues of simplicity, rapidity in analysis, and capacity for real-site detection.

Carbon dots (CDs), which are fluorescent carbon nanomaterials possessing diameters smaller than 10 nm, have captured significant attention after their initial discovery by Xun et al. in 2004 (Xu et al., 2004). CDs have been extensively employed in catalysis (Chen et al., 2024b), bioimaging (Bartkowski et al., 2024), probes (Kayani et al., 2024), anti-counterfeiting (Korah et al., 2023), films (Ding et al., 2024), LEDs (Yuan et al., 2019), lasers (Barman et al., 2023), batteries (Guo et al., 2021), and phototherapy (Shinde et al., 2023), due to their good biocompatibility, high photostability, non-toxicity, excellent water dispersion, environmental friendliness, and low cost (Chelladurai et al., 2024; Delnavaz et al., 2023). Biomass materials, being rich in carbohydrates and proteins, offer a unique avenue for the synthesis of CDs without necessitating passivation or the supplementary addition of nitrogen sources. Their intricate elemental makeup not only confers high aqueous solubility and remarkable optical characteristics but also ensures low toxicity. Presently, numerous investigators have achieved success in synthesizing CDs from a diverse array of biomass materials, including Bael Patra fruit (Vijeata et al., 2022), breadcrumbs (Yin et al., 2023), white pepper (Long et al., 2020), ash of waste paper (Lin et al., 2018), lamb (Zhang et al., 2021), palm oil tree (Abu et al., 2023), tea seed (Wang et al., 2023) and mulberry (Zhou et al., 2022). Although there are increasing reports of successful synthesis of biomass-based CDs for TC detection, for example, Tang, Yu et al. produced fluorescent CDs for TC detection using biomass such as carp roe and dragon fruit peel (Jia et al., 2023; Tang et al., 2022). However, these methods usually require the incorporation of auxiliary additives and the linear range of TC detection is still rather limited. Consequently, the fabrication of a green, real-time and ultrasensitive fluorescent probe for TC detection persists as a significant and worthy research pursuit.

Herein, a novel self-doped nitrogen CDs (N-CDs) fluorescent probe for the inspection of TC in animal-derived foods was constructed by hydrothermal synthesis N-CDs using waste shrimp shell powder as raw material. The presence of TC caused the N-CDs solution's fluorescence intensity to decline sharply, making it possible to quickly identify TC. The detection mechanism was investigated as a fluorescence quenching effect because of the static quenching (SQE) and internal filtration effect (IFE) between the synthesized N-CDs and TC by examining the fluorescence lifespan and Zeta potential. The designed N-CDs probe has outstanding specificity and reproducibility, as demonstrated by the data results of several replicate standard spiked recovery experiments on genuine samples (egg and milk). The results confirmed the efficacy of the fluorescent probe detection approach based on N-CDs, which were even equivalent to those of techniques based on high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). This outcome suggests that the N-CDs obtained from shrimp shells possess substantial potential as a nano-fluorescent probe. The probe is capable of detecting TC with high sensitivity, selectivity, accuracy and real-time.

Shrimp shells were obtained from the Nanning Seafood Processing Plant (Guangxi, China). After being cleaned with water, the leftover shrimp shells were oven-dried for 24 h at 50 °C. Ground dried shrimp shells into a powder and stored at room temperature for backup. TC, amoxicillin (AML), chloramphenicol (CPL), levofloxacin (LEV), ciprofloxacin (CIP), and guaiacol were provided by Beijing Sampo Technology Co., Ltd. Sucrose, glucose (Glc), melamine (MF), fructose (Fru), glutamic acid (Glu) and vitamin C (VC) were acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All metal ions used in the selective experiments were all obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Dialysis bags were manufactured by Hunan Yibo Biotechnology Co., Ltd. The milk and egg samples utilized throughout this work were purchased from area supermarkets. The water employed for the experiments was deionized water.

The N-CDs were synthesized by the following one-step hydrothermal technique: 1.5 g pretreated powdered shrimp shells were dispersed in 20 mL of water, continuously stirring with a rotor for 10 min to mix homogeneously. Next, the blend was moved to a high-pressure autoclave lined with polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) and subjected to reacting at 170 ℃ for 12 h in a thermostat. After the hydrothermal reaction was accomplished, the autoclave was permitted to cool naturally at room temperature. Subsequently, it was taken out and centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 8 min to dislodge impurity substances. The upper liquid was then filtered through a 0.22 μm micron filter to obtain a light brown liquid. Then the filtrate was dialyzed through a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 24 h to obtain an N-CDs solution. Ultimately, the N-CDs solution underwent vacuum freeze-drying to get a solid powder (6.78 % yield was obtained for N-CDs in terms of shrimp shell powder). It was then configured as a standard solution (5 mg/mL) and preserved at 4 °C, avoiding light, for future use. The fabrication of N-CDs is described in Fig. 1.

The relative quantification method determining the QY of shrimp shell-based N-CDs was as follows. Quinine sulfate (54 % QY in 0.10 M sulfuric acid solution at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm) was chosen as the reference control sample. To minimize the reabsorption effect, the ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) absorption intensities of both N-CDs and quinine sulfate solution should be less than 0.05, and the integrated peak areas of the fluorescence emission peaks of the two solutions were measured at the same excitation wavelength. The QY of N-CDs was calculated according to the below formula:
(1)
Y
X
=
Y
S
×
Q
X
Q
S
×
A
S
A
x
×
(
η
X
η
S
)
2
where Y stands for fluorescence QY, Q is the integrated peak area of the measured fluorescence emission peaks,A is absorbance, and η is the refractive index of the liquid being measured. In addition, due to the low concentration of the solutions used in this experiment, the η of both the reference solution and the solution to be measured is approximated to the refractive index of deionized water (η = 1.332). Besides, the subscripts s and x represent the reference solution and the solution to be measured, respectively.

The morphology and particle size of the synthesized N-CDs were characterized by an FEI Tecnai F20 high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, USA). The crystalline structure of N-CDs was analyzed by a DX2700A X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Japan), with a scanning speed of 10 °/min and sweep angles ranging from 10◦ to 80◦. The elemental composition and valence distribution of N-CDs were explored through a Thermo Scientific K-AlpHa Nexsa type X-ray electron spectrometer (XPS, USA). The surface functional groups of N-CDs were examined by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Perkin Elmer, USA), setting the scanning range from 400 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1. The UV-Vis absorption spectra of N-CDs at different wavelengths were measured using a UV-Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2550, Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescence intensity and spectra of N-CDs were determined by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (RF-5301PC, Shimadzu, Japan) with the excitation and emission slits set at 5 nm. The fluorescence lifetime was evaluated by a quantum yield spectrometer (FLS 1000, UK). Zeta potential values were obtained from a Zeta potential analyzer (NANO ZS90, Malvern, UK). The detectability of synthetic N-CDs in real samples was analyzed using HPLC (UltiMate 3000, Column Compartment), and the fluorescence color change of N-CDS was observed by a UV analyzer (Qiwei, China).

Selectivity and anti-interference experiments were performed because other potentially interfering substances may reduce the susceptibility and selectiveness of N-CDs for TC detection, thereby affecting the results of the assay. To assess N-CDs selectivity and sensitivity to TC, 200 μL of TC (1 mM) and 200 μL of the possible interferents (50 mM AML, CPL, LEV, CIP, Glc, MF, Fru, Glu, VC, guaiacol and sucrose, halide solution with 100 mM K+, Na+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Zn2+、Ca2+, Mg2+, sodium salt with Cl–, CO3 2–, OH–, ClO2 –, NO3 –, NO2 –, and SO4 2–) were added to 200 μL of N-CDs, respectively, and diluted to 5 mL with deionized water and sufficiently stirred. A fluorescence spectrometer was used to measure the hybrid's fluorescence, and the emission spectrum of that fluorescence was analyzed and recorded. To assess the N-CDs' anti-interference on TC further, we added the same volume of TC (1 mM) to the system mentioned before, gave it a good shake, and let the mixture stand for 3 min. Then, we plotted the working curves and recorded their fluorescence emission spectra under identical settings once more. Analysis and evaluation of the selectivity and sensitivity of N-CDs to TC could be done by comparing the changes in fluorescence before and after the addition of TC.

The standard assay curves were established by preparing TC solutions with different concentrations. First, 0.48 g (1 mM) of TC was dissolved into 1 L of deionized water to obtain the TC standard solution. A series of 5 mL cuvettes were filled with 200 μL of N-CDs standard solution and variable volumes of solution TC (1 mM), and then the solution was fixed with deionized water so that the final concentrations of TC were 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 15, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 80, 100, 150 and 200 μM, respectively. Using a fluorescence spectrophotometer, the excitation wavelength was set to 350 nm to measure the fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution. The three repetitions of this parallel test were averaged. A standard curve for the TC assay was plotted based on the linear connection between the concentration of TC and the level of fluorescence quenching ((F0-F)/F0) (where F0 and F were before and after the inclusion of TC, respectively).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pre-treatment and assay of real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The milk and egg samples were bought from local supermarkets and preserved in a refrigerator for future use, and the samples were detected by the proposed N-CDs fluorescence probes and traditional HPLC methods simultaneously. The sample pretreatment procedure referred to Liao's method with slight modification (Liao et al., 2021). During the pretreatment process, each sample needed to be thoroughly stirred and mixed well before sampling. Add 2.00 mL of milk and egg stock solution to the centrifuge tube. Next, 5.00 mL of trichloroacetic acid (300 g/L) and 8.00 mL of acetonitrile solution were added for pretreatment. The mixture was stirred until the proteins were fully precipitated, the centrifuge tube was put in the centrifuge and run at 4000 rpm for 5 min. The supernatant of the solution was adjusted to neutral by adding the appropriate amount of NaOH (0.01 M) and then filtrated through a 0.22 μm membrane with a microporous filter. Lastly, the desired sample solution was obtained.

The criteria addition approach was used to assess the performance of the N-CDs fluorescence probe and traditional HPLC methods for TC detection in real samples. To pretreatment samples, known concentrations of TC were applied. The parallel test was repeated and averaged (n = 6). The real samples' spiked recoveries and relative standard deviation (RSD) were determined by substituting the mean fluorescence intensity values into the established standard curve formula.

The global focus has increasingly shifted towards environmental conservation and the pursuit of sustainable development in recent years, as evidenced by a plethora of research and initiatives (Arabi et al., 2021; Arabi et al., 2022; Chen et al., 2024; Hu et al., 2025; Ostovan et al., 2022), shrimp shells—which constitute a substantial part of marine waste, have emerged as a valuable and exploitable natural resource. In the context of the growing interest in the preparation of CDs, shrimp shells, with their abundance and cost-effectiveness, present themselves as an ideal material for the synthesis of high-quality CDs. The high content of proteins and polysaccharides within shrimp shells, following appropriate extraction and pretreatment procedures, serves as excellent precursors. Moreover, their rich composition of trace and mineral elements enables the synthesis of N-CDs with a significant amount of nitrogen and trace elements, thereby enhancing their quality and performance. Consequently, the utilization of shrimp shells for N-CDs synthesis augments the quality of the N-CDs and realizes the noble goal of waste recycling, conferring benefits to both the environment and the local economy.

The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was utilized as a reference index to explore the optimal conditions for synthesizing N-CDs. The experiments were conducted based on controlling a single factor. The effects of three single factors, including the dosage of shrimp shell powder, reaction temperature, and reaction time, were analyzed on N-CDs' fluorescence intensity. The content of fixed solvent water was 20 mL, and the effect of dosage of shrimp shell powder was investigated. The experimental results were illustrated in Fig. S1A, from which it was clear that the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs increased and then declined when the shrimp shell powder was increased. The prepared N-CDs had the strongest fluorescence intensity when the amount of shrimp shell powder added was 1.5 g. Therefore, in the subsequent experiments, we opted for 1.5 g of shrimp shell powder as the raw material for N-CDs synthesis. Furthermore, for the hydrothermal reaction, the reaction time and reaction temperature have a dramatic influence on the fluorescent performance of the synthesized N-CDs. Therefore, it was necessary to investigate the effects of hydrothermal reaction temperature and reaction time on the intensity of N-CDs fluorescence. As can be seen in Fig. S1B, under identical reaction conditions, the intensity of N-CDs rose and then decreased with an increase in reactor temperature, the fluorescence intensity peaked at 170 °C. Ultimately, the impact of reaction duration was analyzed. The assay outcomes were displayed in Fig. S1C, indicating that the synthesized N-CDs had the highest fluorescence intensity at a reaction time of 12 h. In summary, the experimental findings demonstrated that 1.5 g of powdered shrimp shell dispersed in 20 mL of deionized water and reacting at 170 °C for 12 h were optimal conditions for synthesizing N-CDs. The freeze-dried N-CDs synthesized under optimal conditions (QY of 9.72 %) were then configured as a standard solution (5 mg/mL) and preserved at 4 °C, avoiding light, for future use.

Shape and particle size of N-CDs were investigated using HRTEM, which revealed a dispersed, spheroidal-like structure without significant aggregation (
Fig. 2 A). The synthesized N-CDs had an average size of roughly 3.7 nm and a particle size distribution between 2.0 and 6.5 nm ( Fig. 2 B). The crystal lattice pitch of N-CDs was 0.22 nm, which verified the successful syntheses of N-CDs, as indicated in the inset of Fig. 2 A. This lattice spacing was matched to the (100) face of graphitic carbon (Lu et al., 2015). The N-CDs' XRD spectra were displayed in Fig. 2 C. The diffraction peaks were found at 2θ = 22° and 48.9°, respectively. These values could be attributed to the (002) and (101) faces of graphitic carbon, and the prepared N-CDs contained graphitic-like structures (Man et al., 2023). In addition, the XRD pattern showed broad diffraction peaks in the range of 20–24°, which further demonstrated that the synthesized N-CDs consisted predominantly of amorphous graphitic carbon and belonged to conventional N-CDs (Khaledian et al., 2021).

In addition, N-CDs' structural chemical group and elemental distribution were assessed and described using FT-IR and XPS. The transmission spectra of FT-IR (Fig. 2 D) revealed that the broad peak in the region of 3261–3439 cm−1 was associated with the stretching vibration of O-H. Whereas the peak at 3355 cm−1 suggested that N-H was present in the N-CDs. The peak at 860 cm−1 was caused by the aromatic C-H's out-of-plane bending vibration. The peak at 605 cm−1 was caused by the stretching vibration of the C-H in aromatic hydrocarbons, which indicated the presence of the sp2 aromatic structure. The peak in the FT-IR spectra, located at 1547 cm−1, suggested the existence of a bending vibration of CO. The existence of an aromatic ring was shown at the peak of 1080 cm−1, which resulted from the bending vibration of C-H. N-H bending vibration produced the peak at 1356 cm−1. The C-N stretching vibration produced the peak at 1253 cm−1. These results suggested that multiple features of N-doped on the surface of N-CDs were present and N was successfully doped into N-CDs (Liao et al., 2022).

The full XPS spectra, as displayed in Fig. 3 A, revealed three distinctive peaks at 530, 400, and 284 eV, corresponding to O1s, N1s, and C1s, respectively. These peaks suggested that the N-CDs primarily consisted of C, N, and O, with the corresponding atomic percentages of C, N, and O being 67.74 %, 29.52 %, and 7.74 %. Three separate peaks could be fitted to the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (Fig. 3 B) at 284.81, 286.31, and 288.28 eV. These peaks represented several carbon species: sp2 carbon (C-C/CC), oxidized carbon (C-O/CO), and sp3-bonded carbon (C-N), respectively. The peaks at 398.74 eV, 399.70 eV, and 400.59 eV in the high-resolution N1s spectra (Fig. 3 C) corresponded to pyridinic nitrogen (C-N), pyrrolic nitrogen (N-H), and graphitic nitrogen. The presence of C-O and CO was shown by the peaks at 532.58 eV and 531.28 eV in the high-resolution O1s spectra (Fig. 3 D). The produced N-CDs were rich in hydrophilic groups, such as amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups on the surface. They were therefore efficiently diffused in an aqueous solution, according to the XPS results, which were in line with the FT-IR spectroscopic investigations (Liang et al., 2022).

The optical performance of the synthesized N-CDs was evaluated through the study of UV-Vis absorption spectra, emission spectra, and excitation spectra of N-CDs. The UV-Vis absorption spectrum of N-CDs, as depicted in Fig. 4 A, exhibited two discrete absorption peaks at 233 nm and 277 nm, respectively. These absorption peaks could be ascribed to the π-π* and p-p * leaps of the CC bond. For N-CDs, 433 nm was the optimal emission wavelength and 350 nm was the optimal excitation wavelength. Additionally, as displayed in the inset of Fig. 4 A, N-CDs appeared as a light yellow, transparent, clarified solution in daylight, while under UV light (365 nm), the N-CDs solution provided a brilliant blue fluorescence.

Further evaluations were performed to better understand the optical performance of N-CDs. The wavelength dependence of N-CDs through different excitation wavelengths was explored. As shown in Fig. 4 B, N-CDs exhibited both excitation wavelength-independent and excitation-dependent properties. In the range of 300 nm to 350 nm, the emission peak of N-CDs (about 433 nm) hardly changed with the excitation wavelength, and the fluorescence intensity of the emission center of N-CDs increased with the increase of the excitation wavelength. The fluorescence was the strongest at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm, which indicated that the excitation wavelengths of the synthesized N-CDs were wavelength-independent, and they may be explained by a more homogeneous surface state or by a narrower dispersion of the N-CDs' diameter. However, when the excitation wavelength was increased from 350 nm to 450 nm, similar to most of the reported literature (Liu et al., 2024), the emission spectra of N-CDs showed an excitation wavelength-dependent phenomenon, and the fluorescence intensity of the emission centers of N-CDs weakened with the increase of the excitation wavelength. As depicted in the inset of Fig. 4 B, in the excitation range of 300–450 nm, the emission peaks of the normalized fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs were red-shifted from 419 nm by 90 nm to 509 nm, which may be due to the surface defects and the inhomogeneity of the particle size of N-CDs (Feng et al., 2015). This phenomenon coincided with the unique properties of N-CDs, thus proving the successful synthesis of shrimp shell-based N-CDs.

Five parameters were examined to assess the photostability of the N-CDs: pH, salt content, temperature, UV irradiation period, and storage duration. The synthesized N-CDs were shown to have outstanding fluorescence stability in aqueous solutions at pH 3–13, as shown in Fig. S2A. The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was significantly reduced when the pH was raised from 11 to 13 and decreased from 2 to 1. This could be attributed to the functional groups on N-CDs' surfaces being protonated or deprotonated. Furthermore, as the water bath temperature was raised from 30 °C to 90 °C, the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs remained nearly constant (Fig. S2B), demonstrating the N-CDs' exceptional heat tolerance. In a range of NaCl solutions from 0 to 2.0 mM, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs stayed steady ( Fig. S2C). This event suggested that the N-CDs can be employed in an environment with high sodium ions and have good salt resistance. The fact that the fluorescence intensity did not significantly decrease after 60 min of UV irradiation demonstrated the N-CDs' exceptional photostability in Fig. S2D. This was likely because the N-CDs' surface contains carboxyl groups. Besides, as depicted in Fig. S2E, the synthesized N-CDs' fluorescence intensity dropped but remained high and stable for 60 days. It showed the long-term placement and high stability of the synthesized N-CDs. The above data indicated that N-CDs have excellent photostability.
The detection method consistently produced trustworthy and concordance results, with a minimal standard deviation of the detection results for the same TC concentration.

The experimental data indicated that the fluorescence intensity continued to decrease with the addition of TC, and there was no obvious difference under natural light in Fig. 5 A and Fig. S3. As shown in the inset of Fig. 5 A, produced a brilliant blue light when exposed to UV radiation, and the fluorescence of the N-CDs/TC mixed system was quenched. As the concentration of TC increased under UV light, the N-CDs/TC hybrid system's fluorescence progressively decreased. We further explored the link between (F0-F)/ F0 value and TC concentration (Fig. 5 B) and further obtained the linear fitting equation: (F0-F)/F0 = –2.2255 + 1.3224 C
TC , with R2 = 0.9905 (2), indicating a high degree of correlation in Fig. 5 B inset. The LOD of the fluorescence detection method based on N-CDs was determined to be 96 nM using this formula as the standard detection curve and the formula LOD= 3δ/S (Wang et al., 2022), where S is the slope of the linear fitting equation and δ is the standard deviation of the fluorescence intensity of the blank sample (n = 6). The findings revealed that it was quite possible to detect TC residues using synthetic N-CDs. Furthermore, a comprehensive evaluation and incisive comparison were carried out between the fluorescence detection methodology expounded in this study and the extant detection approaches. As delineated in Table S1, the fluorescence detection techniques proffered herein possess an extensive detection range and a remarkably low LOD compared to previous reports, thereby evincing their superiority and potential for practical applications. The impact of latent interference on the TC concentration outcomes was depicted in Fig. 5 C and D. During the experiment, when the interfere levels were substantially greater than the TC concentration, their influence on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was trifling, which indicated that N-CDs were not fluorescently responsive to other ions. Only TC exhibited an obvious quenching at 433 nm under a 350 nm excitation light source (Fig. 5 C). Further, when TC was added again, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs could still be effectively quenched, thoroughly substantiating the outstanding anti-interference capacity of N-CDs (Fig. 5 D). These findings evinced that the probe exhibited eminent selectivity and high specificity in TC detection. Their superior anti-interference performance guaranteed the precision and dependability of the detection results, proffering a potent instrument for the quantitative analysis of TC.

Currently, fluorescence quenching refers to the phenomenon of fluorescence intensity reduction, which mainly includes dynamic quenching (DQE) (Peng et al., 2022), SQE (Pei et al., 2023), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Chen et al., 2022), photoinduced electron transfer (PET) (Jumaah et al., 2022), IFE (Zheng et al., 2021) and aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching (ACQ) (Tang et al., 2023) and other mechanisms. In this work, the fluorescence quenching mechanism was analyzed in detail. According to Fig. 6 A, the excitation and emission peaks of N-CDs were found at 350 nm and 433 nm, respectively, while the UV-Vis absorption spectra of TC revealed two absorption peaks at 274 nm and 358 nm, respectively. A minor fraction of TC's UV-Vis absorption spectra overlapped with the emission spectra of N-CDs, but overall, they overlapped well with the excitation peaks of N-CDs. These findings led to the conclusion that FRET or IFE may be the source of the fluorescence quenching mechanism (Hu et al., 2024). Testing was done on the UV-Vis absorption spectra of TC, N-CDs, and TC/N-CDs mixtures (Fig. 6 B). The findings demonstrated that no new absorption peaks were produced, suggesting that static adsorption was accountable for the quenching of N-CDs’ fluorescence.

The fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs before and after the inclusion of TC were examined to further validate if the FRET was one of the fluorescence quenching mechanisms (Fig. 6 C, D). It was deduced that SQE and the FRET between TC and N-CDs were the causes of the fluorescence quenching process because the corresponding fluorescence lifetimes were 1.121 ns and 1.223 ns, respectively, and they were hardly altered (Liu et al., 2022). Furthermore, the Zeta potentials of N-CDs and TC/N-CDs mixtures were found to be −1.96 mV and −5.84 mV, respectively. Negative potential indicated that the N-CDs’ surfaces were dominated by the hydroxyl and carboxyl radicals, which could dissociate in an aqueous solution to produce negative charges. There was electrostatic attraction between TC and N-CDs, as evidenced by the stark differences in the Zeta potentials of the mixture and N-CDs. Based on the aforementioned experimental findings, we deduced that fluorescence quenching between TC and N-CDs was the result of the IFE and SQE and was dominated by SQE. The fluorescence quenching mechanism was described (Fig. 6 E).

Through rigorous standard addition experiments, the analytical capability of the N-CDs fluorescence probe in real-world situations was thoroughly assessed for practical applications and compared with the traditional HPLC methods. First, known doses of TC were spiked into the milk and egg samples that had been previously treated and detected. The detected se data results by this method, as illustrated in Table 1, showed that the samples' spiked recoveries of varying TC concentrations ranged from 98.2 % to 109.7 %, with a range of RSDs from 1.4 % to 3.2 % (n = 6), which were comparable to the traditional HPLC method. The good accuracy and low RSD data indicated that the fluorescent probe approach based on N-CDs was suitable for identifying TC residues in real samples with dependable results. It was noticed that more than 20 h was needed to analyze a set of real samples by the traditional HPLC method, the as-proposed fluorescent probe approach based on N-CDs only takes about 2 h.

The research presented herein details a one-step hydrothermal methodology for the efficacious synthesis of N-CDs sourced from discarded shrimp shells. Through leveraging the interaction between TC and N-CDs, which encompasses both the IFE and SQE mechanisms, a fluorescent probe detection strategy for TC has been contrived. The probe exhibits remarkable sensitivity and robust anti-interference capabilities, thereby enabling the precise quantification of TC within matrices constituted by milk and eggs. The recovery rate ranged from 98.2 % to 109.7 %, with an RSD spanning 1.4–4.6 %, figures that are commensurate with those obtained via HPLC, further attesting to the accuracy and reproducibility of the proposed assay. Significantly, this N-CDs fluorescent probe-based detection technique is distinguished by several advantageous attributes, such as excellent selectivity, high sensitivity, cost-effective material utilization, environmental benignity, facile preparation, real-time, and short time-consuming. These qualities render it a viable and preferable option for ascertaining the TC content in animal-derived food products.
Supporting Information is available from the author.

Mingying Chen: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Danni Wu: Investigation, Methodology, Software. Xiaoye Deng: Conceptualization, Data curation, Methodology. Junjie Ma: Investigation, Software. Hui Fan: Methodology, Validation. Xiangru Huang: Methodology, Investigation. Peiwen Wang: Software. Yingjie Li: Data curation. Xiufen Liao: Methodology. Lulu Si: Investigation. Congjin Chen: Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31660183), the Guangxi Natural Science Foundation (2025GXNSFAA069247), the Dean/Opening Project of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Petrochemical Resource Processing and Process Intensification Technology (2024K002). The authors would like to thank Jianliang Liao from Shiyania Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS measurement.
